posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Brandon R:

Come on, Jon, you know very well it’s not the fact that it’s 100% new oak, but how that oaks shows in the final wine. The pitchfork wielders come out only when that oak is super evident in the wine. I have a sneaking suspicion that DRC might know a thing or two about managing that oak. Just maybe.

Brandon,

I don’t believe Jon is blowing anything out of proportion at this point. There are multiple instances where people take offense at new Oak with wines, even if it is shown that the proportion is relatively small.

I totally agree with you that it’s not the percentage of new Oak but how well it is integrated. And much of that has to do with the toasting of the barrels themselves, the makeup of the fruit itself, and how long the wine sees in Oak.

But again, I don’t think he’s out of line whatsoever.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:
Brandon R:

Come on, Jon, you know very well it’s not the fact that it’s 100% new oak, but how that oaks shows in the final wine. The pitchfork wielders come out only when that oak is super evident in the wine. I have a sneaking suspicion that DRC might know a thing or two about managing that oak. Just maybe.

Brandon,

I don’t believe Jon is blowing anything out of proportion at this point. There are multiple instances where people take offense at new Oak with wines, even if it is shown that the proportion is relatively small.

I totally agree with you that it’s not the percentage of new Oak but how well it is integrated. And much of that has to do with the toasting of the barrels themselves, the makeup of the fruit itself, and how long the wine sees in Oak.

And much of it has to do with the wine that’s going in the barrels. I expect new oak on Cal cabs and Bordeaux, and on top-flight red and white Burgundies. That tends to integrate with time. I generally don’t like much or any oak on Barolo or Barbaresco. Whatever appeal it sometimes gives wines up front, it tends to mess up the aromatics with age and make the wines imprecise, I find. Same thing with syrah in the Northern Rhone (except that I’ve enjoyed Guigal’s heavily oaked Cote Roties).

I go for my pitchforks when it feels like the wine has been tarted up with new oak to win points.

posts.name: François Audouze

“How would we know? Most of the DRC in the market is probably fake!”

based on what ?
Some years ago, when bottles of domaine Ponsot were withdrawn of a sale because they were obviously fakes, Mr. Ponsot declared : “today, 80% of Burgundies on the market are fakes”. He had not the slightest idea about the actual situation and he put an abnormal suspicion on the market.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Thomas,

Mel- could it also be a lighter toast ?

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Edward,

When a winemaker called us up and asked for a light toast, our first question was: when did you get back from Burgundy?

Burgundian winemakers sometimes tell people they use light toast, but in reality it’s not so. Henri Jayer used to tell people he used light toast, but looking at the barrels being made, I would have said heavy.

Another key point is that the longer you season the wood, the more subtle the toast appears, regardless of toasting regime.

posts.name: Brandon J.
posts.cooked:
That’s fascinating.

I personally couldn’t care less how much or little new oak is used but if it clouds the grapes/nose I’ll go on 10 minute tangents despite the chagrin of the misses.

So what do you do as a winemaker to combat that? I recently tried a 100% new oak White Rose Pinot and was shocked it was 100%. I had just tried a pinot that was 20% new from another producer and it tasted far more oaky than the White Rose.

Is it purely cooperage? Or extended aging? Fruit that can take it? Combination of many things?

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Brandon,

You are right. It is a combination of many things, such as the origin of the oak, the seasoning of the wood, the pH, TA and alcohol levels. I think if you put pinot into barrel right after primary fermentation, the wine has a better chance of marrying with the wood right away.I think wines with less than 14% alc and good acidity stand up to oak better. Then there is the question of cellar temperature.

When I made Ici la Bas Oregon Pinot with Jim Clendenen, we found that those wines did very well with 100% new oak, but that the Anderson Valley wines tended to do better with half thata,tho we made Chardonnay with one vineyard that was fantastic with 100% new.

Of course, a lot of it depends on when you plan to drink it/sell it. The advantage of being DRC is that nobody gets their 2015s and says, Boy, let’s pop open a bottle tonight. Every body waits at least ten years.

Heavy toast can impart sweetness, rather than toastiness, and that can fool people.

There is also the psychological side of it. If I am selling our Uvaggio Vermentino, a wine that gets its kiss of oak when the hose goes through the barrel cellar, people who know what I did for a living sometimes find it oaky.

posts.name: Brandon Tebbe
posts.cooked:
Mel,

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:

Time in barrel depends on the quality of the vintage but usually at least 16 months

I haven’t been there since the pandemic so you have to remember they are always changing

posts.name: John Morris

There is also the psychological side of it. If I am selling our Uvaggio Vermentino, a wine that gets its kiss of oak when the hose goes through the barrel cellar, people who know what I did for a living sometimes find it oaky.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I will see the GM of the cooperage next week and will ask.

When I was working full-time, winemakers would call up and say, I want a light toast. My first question was, when did you get back from Burgundy?? Somebody in Oregon told me Henri Jayer used light toast. I had pictures of their barrel being made…very dark on the insides.

Andre Noblet told Zelma Long and me that there was just a kiss of toast on the insides. Later, I saw the barrels being made. I would have called it medium, maybe medium plus.

Something that is crucial in understanding barrel making and its influence on wine flavor: longer the seasoning of the wood —done in the right places of course–the more subtle the oak flavor and toastiness in the wine.

Where the wood is seasoned is crucial. You want lots of wind and rain. The wood is not just drying but is changing. Hot spells are not good which always made me worry about wood that was seasoned during hot and dry years like 2003.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
I don’t think folks in general understand the ‘variability’ in the production of oak - thanks for your insight, my friend.

And thanks for clarifying that ‘light toast’ does not necessarily equate to less ‘oak presence’ in a wine . . .

Cheers

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I did not realise that this was a post that was started seven years ago!

Re light toast:
1/the wood has to be well seasoned…otherwise the wine can taste harshly tannic
2/how the wine is made is also crucial…racking ‘clean’ wine into new barrels can be a disaster…more rough tannins

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
I did participate at an oak seminar held by Taransaud years ago. Very educational. Same wine, different barrels. Wood from France, US, Slavonia. Well seasoned and less well. Light, medium, dark toast. Not surprising: very different results. After that experience the conclusion was that we drink more oak than wine. Surely a bit overblown. But only a bit. The oak is a major factor of the taste and quality of a barrel aged wine.

BTW: I thought the seasoning plays a bigger role than the toasting when it comes to the quality of the finished wine. The oak will be better integrated and the tannins smoother.

posts.cooked: I don´t know. This was part of a Wine Seminar at Villa ´d Este in Italy.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: So the suggestion is to rack dirty in the new oak barrels rather than settle the press results? Interesting . . .

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
What is they say in the Bible, Don’t put old ideas into new goatskins??

Let me add a little historical perspective. Back in the 70s virtually all reds were fermented in tanks, racked,put through ML in tank, and then they would go into barrels after Christmas. Dick Graff came back from Burgundy with the idea of racking the wine right after fermentation. He might settle the press results but that is all. ML took place in barrel. He got much better oak integration. This was radical for the time. In the mid 80s winemakers with a shortage of tank space started to do the same thing with Bordeaux reds. When I toured Bordeaux around 1987 with Dave Ramey and John Hawley, they mentioned that they were doing this. Everyone thought this was whacko. Five years later Pascal Chattonet spread this idea around Bordeaux. This is good and bad for the coopers because nobody in the Northern Hemisphere wants barrels after Xmas.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Juergen:

Was this a Grand Jury thing??
I remember being invited to Villa D Este but I think I sent my assistant.

Taransaud puts a lot of effort into these seminars.I think the last one we did here was in 2015. I arranged an experiment with different lengths of wood drying. Everyone said they wanted to attend but there were around 40 no shows out of 150 invitees…I don’t think they ever did one here on that scale again.

Karl K:

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: They have a larger percentage of non-François wood than they used to, notably from Lagrange, a part of the Berthomieu group.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
I’ve held barrels an extra year before using them on quite a few occasions, and have used barrels as many as 4 years after being coopered.

For Chassin 500L puncheons, an extra year of sitting can bring a bit more restraint to the wines and moderate the impact of the oak. They will only use two season staves in the barrels, so even as 500L puncheons the barrels tend to be high impact. The extra year moderates the volume and sweetness a bit, and I definitely feel the results of holding these barrels for a year are very good.

The Chassin barrels are very well made, and also tend to really hit their stride after being filled 1-2 times(20 months per fill, rather than 11). So I would prefer to hold them vs using them.

I used 4 Damy barrels (225L) that were coopered in 2014 in 2017(filled with Whistling Ridge fruit). At that length of time they present very differently, leaning far more into dry baking spice notes, adding much less sweetness and volume, and really having a more pronounced tannic presence for the first 12-13 months of elevage. As the wines were in them around 18 months, the tannins really settled down, and they shifted from a sort of “fun” outlier into being a really nice component of the final blend. They were balanced by 3 Chassin 225L barrels that I bought new(the only time I have bought that size new and used them immediately) that produced dense, intense, and somewhat Dr. Pepper-y flavors for the first 15 months and then like magic started to integrate as we reached the second Spring before bottling. Quite literally Megan hated them, and to some extent myself as well, up until March before bottling. I was disappointed, but was tasting through in March and realized they had taken a serious shift in direction. Over the next month, the three Chassin barrels slimmed down quite a bit, and the Damy older barrels really added some weight, while retaining the beatiful dry cinnamon/all-spice notes that really marked the older “new” barrels. Both sets wound up be a significant part of our first block bottlings.

And in 2020, we didn’t use any of the new barrels for Pinot Noir or Chardonnay due to the smoke from the fires. Barrels had been ordered that soring though, and arrived in August. In 2021 we had a very small set, so I used both 2020 coopered new puncheons and 2021 coopered new puncheons for both 2021, and then 2020,2021, and 2022 coopered wood in 2022. Both vintages are excellent and the variety adds some layers, IMO, as the differing levels of barrel age bring different things to the wines.

That said, new puncheons that used to be €1100 are now pushing €1700-1900, and barrels as much as €1400. Investing €1700 to sit on it for 12 months before using it and then waiting 20 more months before getting any return is not a good business model…

participants.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
